meta.homeTitle: Lucas Attali · Fullstack developer and entrepreneur
meta.homeDescription: Lucas Attali, fullstack developer between the Mediterranean and the Pacific. Co-founder of Beavers and Skalp. Next.js, React, SvelteKit, Svelte, TypeScript, SEO, web development and e-commerce.
meta.aboutDescription: Fullstack developer and entrepreneur in the south of France, co-founder of Beavers and Skalp, Sydney on the horizon. Every detail held, from mockup to production.
meta.experienceDescription: Lucas Attali's career: more than ten years in web, from technical project manager to agency co-founder. SEO, development, Next.js, React, SvelteKit and TypeScript stack.
meta.workDescription: Lucas Attali's work: SEO and development results at Beavers, the build of Skalp, and a netlinking SaaS in the works.
meta.rallyTitle: Rally, real-time match companion · Lucas Attali
meta.rallyDescription: A real-time match companion: live updates, predictions for fun and a multiplayer leaderboard. Playable demo, Vercel front and event-driven AWS backend (Lambda, WebSocket, DynamoDB).

about.aboutLede: Fullstack developer and entrepreneur, I build web products from the south of France. Beavers by day, Skalp on the side, and a product of my own in the works. My obsession fits in a few words: every detail held, from the first mockup to production.
about.aboutP1: I learned the web through engineering, then product, then running a company. Today I co-found Beavers, the agency I started in 2019 with my wife, where I handle SEO (strategy, article writing, netlinking), development (websites, landing pages, contest games) and GTM tracking, for agencies and their clients or directly for clients.
about.aboutPSkalp: With my wife too, I co-found and run Skalp, a single-product e-commerce I build end to end. And right now, I'm developing a product of my own: a SaaS for netlinking specialists, not yet released.
about.aboutP2: The way I work hasn't changed since my first site: clean foundations, typed code, clear separation of concerns, and nothing approximate left behind. 'Details make perfection' isn't a decorative quote here, it's the method.
about.aboutP3: Off-screen, I live near the Mediterranean, I'm slowly doing up a house, and I spend most of my time with my people and on a padel court (left side, always). A shiba inu supervises. And on the horizon, one fixed idea that steers the rest: moving to Sydney.

rally.lede: A real-time match companion. You follow the game play by play, make predictions for fun, and climb the leaderboard. The first demo runs on a replayable match: France vs Australia, 2019 FIBA World Cup.
rally.prodIntro: No streaming, just the essentials of the game live and something to play with. The idea: turn a passive viewer into an active player, no money, just for fun.
rally.f1Desc: score, quarter and clock, derived from the engine continuously
rally.f2Desc: the flow of the game at a glance, the visual signature
rally.f3Desc: who scores next, who wins the quarter
rally.f4Desc: your progress against bots and other players, live
rally.f5Desc: the action feed, in real time
rally.archIntro: Static front served from the CDN, event-driven AWS backend, with two real-time mechanics. A shared clock, derived from a server anchor, makes every visitor see the same second of the match. And a WebSocket relays player presence and predictions live, without the server computing any score: each client derives the same leaderboard on its own, bit for bit, deterministic bots included. If the anchor doesn't respond, it gracefully falls back to a local replay: the site never breaks.
rally.awsLambda: exposes the match anchor (Function URL) and drives the real-time room: connect, join, predict
rally.awsWs: real time: player presence and predictions relayed to everyone, live
rally.awsDdb: the memory: shared anchor, active connections and unique handles
rally.awsEb: the guardian: watches and regenerates the anchor continuously (self-heal)
rally.awsIac: the whole infra described in YAML, deployed with one command
rally.awsFront: static front, clock synced to the server, local replay fallback
rally.engineNote: Event-sourced engine in pure TypeScript, seeded bots and a leaderboard derived bit for bit on the client, with no server-side scoring. Infra as SAM (IaC, eu-west-3), WebSocket in production. Next step: harden prediction validation server-side, then migrate to CDK.
rally.statusBody: V1 runs on a fixed France vs Australia match, which decouples it from any sports API. It's all there already: shared clock, live player presence, and a multiplayer leaderboard where everyone sees the same result. Next: a real sports data source, player accounts and more badges. It's my playground to push real time and AWS in real conditions.
